summary: DeVault documents the technical work required to bring VR support to Wayland through DRM leasing, a mechanism that lets the Wayland compositor hand off direct display control to VR applications for minimal latency. The work involved drafting a Wayland protocol extension, implementing it in wlroots/sway, creating a new Vulkan extension and its Mesa implementation, and adding Xwayland compatibility. The project was sponsored by Status.im as part of building a Wayland-driven VR workspace. Each layer of the stack required careful design decisions around security, backwards compatibility with X11, and keeping the protocol simple rather than accommodating legacy systems.
key_points: DRM leasing allows a Wayland compositor to delegate direct display control to VR clients, bypassing the display server for lower latency
The work spanned the entire graphics stack: Wayland protocol, wlroots/sway compositor, Vulkan WSI extension, Mesa driver implementation, and Xwayland compatibility
Security concerns about malicious clients reading unauthorized framebuffers were resolved by demonstrating the kernel rejects such attempts
The Xwayland integration was the most difficult part due to a design mismatch between the X11 Vulkan extension's mode querying assumptions and the simpler Wayland protocol
DeVault deliberately kept the Wayland protocol simpler than the X11 equivalent, refusing to add complexity for legacy compatibility
money_quotes: The rub of this idea is that the Wayland compositor, the DRM master, can 'lease' some of its resources to a client so they can drive your display directly.
For a number of reasons, minimizing latency being an important one, VR applications prefer to do these tasks directly rather than be routed through the display server like most applications are.
The latter was proven to be a non-issue by writing a malicious client and demonstrating that the kernel rejects its attempts to do evil.
I found this distasteful, because it was making the protocol more complex for the sake of a legacy system.
In the end, it turned out to be much simpler than the corresponding X version.
key_insight: Bringing VR to Wayland required threading a new capability through every layer of the Linux graphics stack, but the result is a cleaner and simpler design than the equivalent X11 approach.
